reason: Both give a clear take-it-conditionally recommendation with reasonable math (17% dilution, 8%/mo growth to ~75k MRR) and tie the decision to use of funds and founder ambition. The key differentiator is the default-alive framing: the rubric stresses that default-alive means choosing acceleration not survival, with leverage on the founder's side. Answer B explicitly and correctly uses this (term sheet plus default-alive status equals leverage, suggesting shopping or pushing price), while Answer A frames things with more urgency/fragility (thin runway margin, money later bought at desperation, take it if actually default-dead), which understates the leverage point and leans toward survival-mode framing. Answer A is stronger on term mechanics (board seat, 1x non-participating pref, pro-rata, protective provisions). Both are concise and actionable, but B better captures the central conceptual insight the rubric calls out.
